params.text:
Raising the alarm and calling 999

If you discover a fire, act immediately and calmly:

  • Raise the alarm straight away by operating the nearest manual call point (break-glass) and alerting colleagues. Getting people out and summoning help matter far more than tackling the fire.
  • Call 999 and ask for the fire service. Give the full address, tell them it is a care home, and say if anyone is trapped or has restricted mobility. Do not assume someone else has called.
  • Follow your evacuation plan – move people away from the fire to a place of safety, close doors behind you to slow the spread, and use residents' PEEPs.
  • Do not use lifts, and only fight the fire with an extinguisher if it is very small, safe to do so, and you have a clear exit.
  • Go to the assembly point, help with the roll call, and never go back in for belongings.

params.alt: Illustration for If a fire starts

params.text:
The role of fire marshals and wardens

Many care settings appoint trained fire marshals (sometimes called fire wardens) to lead the response. Their duties include raising and confirming the alarm, coordinating the evacuation, checking rooms, toilets and communal areas so no one is left behind, ensuring fire doors are closed, and carrying out a headcount or roll call at the assembly point. Between emergencies they help with routine checks of exits, signage, extinguishers and alarms. Even if you are not a marshal, you must know who yours are and follow their instructions during an evacuation. Attend every fire drill – drills are how the whole team practises the plan safely.

params.taskDescription: Drag the words into the gaps to complete this summary of what to do in a fire.
params.textField:
If you find a fire, first *raise* the alarm, then call *999* for the fire service.
To operate an extinguisher, remember the word *PASS*.
During evacuation you should close *doors* behind you and never use the *lift*.
Once outside, go to the assembly point and never go *back* inside.

params.question: If the fire alarm sounds, it is fine to go back to a resident's room to collect their handbag before leaving.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: No. You must never go back for belongings in a fire. Focus only on getting people to a place of safety and let the fire service do their job.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Never re-enter or delay for belongings. Getting people to safety is the only priority – possessions can be replaced, lives cannot.
